skill.name: python-venv-manager
skill.description: Guides Python virtual environment setup, dependency management, and project environment troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Set up and maintain isolated Python development environments with venv, Poetry, Pipenv, and pyenv.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and environment variable patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows this is a documentation-only Python environment skill with no bundled executable code or confirmed malicious intent, but several examples teach risky shell patterns that should be published with warnings.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The skill includes pyenv installation examples that pipe curl output directly into bash. This is a common install pattern, but it executes remote code without integrity verification, so users should review installer sources or use package-manager alternatives.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-bash pattern appears twice in install instructions. The context is legitimate setup documentation, so this is risky guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Bulk Dependency Migration Commands
medium_findings.description: Several migration examples pass dependency file content through command substitution, xargs, or unquoted shell variables. Malformed requirement lines can be split, globbed, or interpreted as unintended arguments to Poetry.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real shell examples operating on dependency files. They do not show direct credential theft or arbitrary command execution, but they are fragile with untrusted requirements content.

medium_findings.title: Secret Configuration Examples Require Care
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates creating .env files, loading SECRET_KEY and DATABASE_URL, and selecting production database settings from environment variables. This is normal application configuration guidance, but it involves secrets and should emphasize local-only storage and secret rotation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly reference .env files and sensitive configuration names. The skill also says never to commit .env files, so this is a moderate handling concern, not evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Commands Are Core To The Skill
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are expected because the skill teaches venv, pip, Poetry, Pipenv, pyenv, pytest, and Docker workflows. The commands are visible examples, not hidden scripts bundled with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation with explicit command snippets. Broad command execution remains a user-safety concern because an agent may run commands on behalf of a user.

low_findings.title: Hidden And Temporary Paths Are Legitimate Examples
low_findings.description: The .pyenv, .venv, .env, .pre-commit-config.yaml, and /tmp paths are used as normal Python tooling and Docker examples. No evidence found that the skill reads hidden files for exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path usage aligns with documented Python environment conventions. The static filesystem findings are mostly false positives in this context.

low_findings.title: Static Critical Docker Cleanup Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The recursive delete finding points to apt cache cleanup in a Dockerfile example. It removes /var/lib/apt/lists after package installation, not root or a user home directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding Dockerfile context confirms this is a standard image-size cleanup pattern. The static label does not match the actual path or intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Output Executed By bash
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern confirmed in install examples. It should be treated as risky unless the installer source is trusted and reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and semantically confirmed. It is common but still dangerous when copied or run automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unquoted Shell Expansion From Dependency Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern confirmed in migration examples that convert requirements into Poetry dependencies. Safer approaches should parse requirement files or review package names first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use command substitution, xargs, and unquoted variables with dependency file content. The risk depends on input trust and execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Review Completed With No Evidence Found
dangerous_patterns.description: No suspicious instructions such as override commands, requests to skip analysis, pre-approval claims, or system-message impersonation were found in SKILL.md.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review for prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. The opening instruction defines the skill role but does not attempt to override marketplace audit rules.

content.value_statement: Python projects often fail because environments, dependency files, and tool versions drift. This skill guides setup, migration, and troubleshooting for reproducible Python workflows.
actual_capabilities: Compares venv, virtualenv, Poetry, Pipenv, and pyenv workflows.
Provides setup steps for new and existing Python projects.
Shows dependency locking, requirements files, and Poetry migration patterns.
Covers pytest, linting, formatting, type checking, and pre-commit setup.
Offers troubleshooting guidance for conflicts, import errors, SSL issues, and permissions.
Includes Docker examples for Python virtual environment builds.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include tested automation scripts.
Some shell examples should be reviewed before execution in production repositories.
It does not resolve dependencies automatically without the chosen Python tool.
It cannot verify private package indexes or organization-specific policies.

use_cases.title: Start A New Python Project
use_cases.description: Create an isolated environment, install common tools, and write dependency files for a clean project start.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Environments
use_cases.description: Choose Poetry, Pipenv, or pip-tools conventions and document repeatable setup commands for contributors.

use_cases.title: Repair Broken Dependencies
use_cases.description: Diagnose version conflicts, import failures, corrupted environments, and platform-specific installation problems.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic venv
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Python virtual environment for this project. Include activation commands, pip upgrade steps, and a simple requirements.txt workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Choose A Dependency Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare venv, Poetry, Pipenv, and pip-tools for my project. Recommend one option and explain the setup steps I should use.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate To Poetry
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate this Python project from requirements.txt to Poetry. Identify risks, preserve dev dependencies, and outline validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Environment Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Python environment failure and propose a safe recovery plan. Check interpreter version, dependency conflicts, lock files, and platform-specific causes.

output_examples.input: I need a clean setup for a small Python service.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step environment plan with a recommended tool, dependency file strategy, activation notes, and verification checks.

output_examples.input: My Poetry migration fails after reading requirements.txt.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering requirement parsing, incompatible pins, dev dependency separation, and lock file regeneration.

output_examples.input: Our team has inconsistent Python versions.
output_examples.output: A standardization plan using a project Python version file, documented setup commands, and contributor validation steps.

best_practices: Review shell commands before running them, especially installer scripts and migration commands.
Use one isolated environment per project and keep environment folders out of version control.
Commit lock files when reproducible application installs are required.
anti_patterns: Do not install project dependencies into the global Python environment.
Do not commit .env files, virtual environment folders, or local cache directories.
Do not run curl-to-shell installers without checking the source and trust model.

faq.question: Does this skill create virtual environments automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and guidance that an AI tool or user can apply in a project workspace.

faq.question: Which Python tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers venv, virtualenv, Poetry, Pipenv, pyenv, pip-tools, pytest, linting tools, and Docker workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe to run every command directly?
faq.answer: No. Review commands first, especially remote installers, dependency migration commands, and commands that modify files.

faq.question: Can it help migrate requirements.txt to Poetry?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes migration guidance, but complex or untrusted requirement files need manual review.

faq.question: Does it manage production secrets?
faq.answer: No. It explains environment variable patterns, but secret storage and rotation must follow your platform policy.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools for applying the guidance.
